questions.question:
Consider the following relation:

Student table:
Roll_No | Student_Name
1       | Raj
2       | Rohit
3       | Raj

Performance table:
Roll_No | Course  | Marks
1       | Math    | 80
1       | English | 70
2       | Math    | 75
3       | English | 80
3       | Physics | 65
3       | Math    | 80

Consider the following SQL query:
SELECT S.Student_Name, Sum(P.Marks)
FROM Student S, Performance P
WHERE S.Roll_No = P.Roll_No
GROUP BY S.Student_Name

The number of rows that will be returned by the SQL query is _________________.
questions.to_find: Number of rows returned by the SQL query with GROUP BY Student_Name

understand.plain: We have two tables: Student (with Roll_No and Student_Name) and Performance (with Roll_No, Course, and Marks). The SQL joins them on Roll_No, then groups results by Student_Name and sums the marks. Notice that two students (Roll_No 1 and 3) are both named 'Raj', so after grouping by name they form a single group.

keywords.explain: Aggregates rows that have the same value in the specified column into a single result row.
keywords.example: GROUP BY Student_Name collapses all rows where Student_Name = 'Raj' into one row.

keywords.explain: Combines rows from two tables where the join condition (here S.Roll_No = P.Roll_No) is satisfied.
keywords.example: Roll_No 1 in Student joins with all Performance rows where Roll_No = 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='510' height='210' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>After JOIN + GROUP BY Student_Name</text><rect x='20' y='45' width='220' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='65' class='tag ink'>Student_Name</text><text x='200' y='65' class='tag ink'>Sum(Marks)</text><rect x='20' y='75' width='220' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='95' class='sub ink'>Raj (Roll 1 + Roll 3)</text><text x='200' y='95' class='sub ink'>375</text><rect x='20' y='105' width='220' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='125' class='sub ink'>Rohit (Roll 2)</text><text x='200' y='125' class='sub ink'>75</text><text x='20' y='165' class='sub mut'>Raj: 80+70+80+65+80 = 375</text><text x='20' y='185' class='sub mut'>Rohit: 75</text><text x='20' y='205' class='sub ok'>Total rows returned = 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: After joining Student and Performance on Roll_No and grouping by Student_Name, only two groups exist: 'Raj' (covers Roll_No 1 and 3, summing all their marks = 375) and 'Rohit' (Roll_No 2, sum = 75). So 2 rows are returned.

given.aim: Find the number of rows returned after joining Student and Performance tables and grouping by Student_Name.

terms.meaning: Has 3 rows: Roll_No 1 (Raj), 2 (Rohit), 3 (Raj). Note Roll_No 1 and 3 share the same name 'Raj'.
terms.example: Two different roll numbers map to the same student name 'Raj'.
terms.connects: This causes both Roll_No 1 and Roll_No 3 records to fall into the same GROUP BY bucket.

terms.meaning: Has 6 rows: Roll_No 1 has 2 courses, Roll_No 2 has 1 course, Roll_No 3 has 3 courses.
terms.example: Roll_No 3 has courses English (80), Physics (65), Math (80).
terms.connects: After joining, the 6 performance rows are distributed across the two name groups.

terms.meaning: Collapses all joined rows with the same Student_Name into one output row with aggregate Sum(Marks).
terms.example: All rows with Student_Name = 'Raj' become one output row.
terms.connects: Determines the final row count: one row per distinct Student_Name value.

given.plan: First perform the join (S.Roll_No = P.Roll_No) to get 6 rows. Then identify distinct Student_Name values in the joined result. The number of distinct names equals the number of output rows.

steps.title: Perform the join
steps.apply:
Join Student and Performance on Roll_No:
- Roll_No 1 (Raj) joins with (Math,80) and (English,70) -> 2 rows with Student_Name='Raj'
- Roll_No 2 (Rohit) joins with (Math,75) -> 1 row with Student_Name='Rohit'
- Roll_No 3 (Raj) joins with (English,80), (Physics,65), (Math,80) -> 3 rows with Student_Name='Raj'
Total: 6 joined rows.
steps.note: Both Roll_No 1 and Roll_No 3 produce rows with Student_Name='Raj'.

steps.title: Apply GROUP BY Student_Name
steps.apply:
Distinct Student_Name values in the 6 joined rows:
- 'Raj': 5 rows (2 from Roll 1 + 3 from Roll 3) -> 1 output row, Sum(Marks) = 80+70+80+65+80 = 375
- 'Rohit': 1 row -> 1 output row, Sum(Marks) = 75
Number of output rows = 2.
steps.note: The key insight: GROUP BY uses the name string, not Roll_No. Since two different Roll_No values share the same name 'Raj', they collapse into one group.

solution.result: 2 rows are returned by the SQL query.

questions.formula_note: GROUP BY aggregation collapses all rows with the same group-key value into one output row; the number of output rows equals the number of distinct group-key values in the joined relation.

questions.question:
Consider the following relation Cinema(theater, address, capacity).

Which of the following options will be needed at the end of the SQL query

SELECT P1.address
FROM Cinema P1

such that it always finds the addresses of theaters with maximum capacity?

A. WHERE P1.capacity >= All (select P2.capacity from Cinema P2)
B. WHERE P1.capacity >= Any (select P2.capacity from Cinema P2)
C. WHERE P1.capacity >= All (select max(P2.capacity) from Cinema P2)
D. WHERE P1.capacity >= Any (select max(P2.capacity) from Cinema P2)
questions.answer: A. WHERE P1.capacity >= All (select P2.capacity from Cinema P2)
questions.to_find: The WHERE clause that correctly retrieves addresses of theaters with maximum capacity

understand.plain: We have a Cinema table with theater name, address, and capacity. We want to find the address(es) of the theater(s) with the highest capacity. The question asks which SQL WHERE clause correctly expresses this condition.

keywords.explain: 'x >= ALL (subquery)' is true if x is greater than or equal to every value returned by the subquery. This means x must be the maximum.
keywords.example: 5 >= ALL(3,4,5) is TRUE; 4 >= ALL(3,4,5) is FALSE.

keywords.explain: 'x >= ANY (subquery)' is true if x is greater than or equal to at least one value in the subquery. This means x is at least the minimum.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='470' height='170' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Cinema capacities: 100, 200, 300</text><text x='20' y='60' class='sub ink'>capacity >= ALL(100,200,300) ?</text><rect x='20' y='70' width='120' height='25' class='no'/><text x='30' y='88' class='tag' fill='white'>100: FALSE</text><rect x='160' y='70' width='120' height='25' class='no'/><text x='170' y='88' class='tag' fill='white'>200: FALSE</text><rect x='300' y='70' width='120' height='25' class='ok'/><text x='310' y='88' class='tag' fill='white'>300: TRUE</text><text x='20' y='120' class='sub ink'>capacity >= ANY(100,200,300) ?</text><rect x='20' y='130' width='120' height='25' class='ok'/><text x='30' y='148' class='tag' fill='white'>100: TRUE</text><rect x='160' y='130' width='120' height='25' class='ok'/><text x='170' y='148' class='tag' fill='white'>200: TRUE</text><rect x='300' y='130' width='120' height='25' class='ok'/><text x='310' y='148' class='tag' fill='white'>300: TRUE</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: For Cinema with capacities 100, 200, 300: 'capacity >= ALL' passes only 300 (the maximum), while 'capacity >= ANY' passes all three (all are >= the minimum 100). So >= ALL correctly selects only the maximum-capacity theater(s).

given.aim: Find the correct WHERE clause to select only theaters whose capacity is the maximum among all theaters.

terms.meaning: Relation with theater name, its address, and its seating capacity.
terms.connects: We want to select address values where capacity is maximum.

terms.meaning: The value must be >= every single value produced by the subquery, meaning it equals the maximum.
terms.example: 300 >= ALL(100,200,300) is true; equivalent to capacity = max(capacity).
terms.connects: This is exactly the condition needed to identify maximum-capacity theaters.

terms.meaning: The value must be >= at least one value in the subquery, meaning it is >= the minimum.
terms.example: 100 >= ANY(100,200,300) is true, so this would return all theaters.
terms.connects: This is too weak a condition — it does not isolate the maximum.

given.plan: Evaluate each option by understanding what ALL vs ANY means. Use the definition: x >= ALL(S) iff x = max(S). Check options C and D: subquery 'select max(P2.capacity)' returns a single scalar, so >= ALL and >= ANY of a single value are equivalent and both correct, but option A is the standard canonical form.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A: >= ALL (full subquery)
steps.apply:
Option A: WHERE P1.capacity >= ALL (select P2.capacity from Cinema P2)
The subquery returns all capacity values in Cinema. P1.capacity >= ALL of them means P1.capacity is at least as large as every capacity, i.e., it is the maximum capacity. This is correct.
steps.note: This is the standard SQL idiom for finding the maximum using ALL quantifier.

steps.title: Evaluate Options B, C, D
steps.apply:
Option B: >= ANY returns true for any theater with capacity >= the minimum capacity, i.e., all theaters. Incorrect.
Option C: >= ALL (select max(...)) — subquery returns one value (the max). >= ALL of a single-element set means >= that value, so this also works. However, it is redundant/unusual.
Option D: >= ANY (select max(...)) — subquery returns one value. >= ANY of a single value = >= that value. Also technically works, but unusual.
Among the given options, Option A is the most direct, canonical, and always-correct answer.
steps.note: Option A is the intended correct answer as it directly uses the ALL quantifier over the full table, which is the textbook SQL pattern for maximum selection.

solution.result: Option A: WHERE P1.capacity >= All (select P2.capacity from Cinema P2)

questions.formula_note: SQL ALL quantifier: 'x >= ALL(subquery)' is equivalent to 'x = MAX of subquery values', making it the correct clause to find theaters with maximum capacity.
